PROJECT

Version 3.0

Prepared By  Energy and Environment Consultancy Joint Stock Company  
Contact  Address: 8th Floor, Diamond Flower Tower, No. 48 Le Van Luong

Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
4 
1 PROJECT DETAILS 
1.1 Summary Description of the Project 
The Soc Son waste to power plant project (hereinafter “the project”) includes the construction 
and operation of a waste treatment plant, which is located at Nam Son waste treatment complex 
area, Soc Son District, Hanoi. The project is owned by Ha Noi Thien  Y Environmental Energy Joint 
Stock Company, which was established from 3 investors including Soc Son Renewable Energy 
Joint Stock Company, Perfect Wave Holdings Limited Company and Europe Tianying BVBA 
Company. 
The purpose of the project activity is to tr eat 4,000 tons of MSW per day to generate electricity 
with the maximum capacity of 75 MW. However, the plant is designed and installed with the 
capacity of 90 MW for the following reasons:  
- The nature of the waste to energy (WtE)  plant is unstable operation due to the harsh working 
conditions. The equipment requires more frequent maintenance/repair while the 
maintenance/repair time is relatively long compared to other conventional thermal power 
plants.  
- The waste treatment plant m ust be operated continuously. The reduction of operating 
capacity (partial shutdown of the plant for maintenance) will cause garbage congestion and 
environmental problems.  
Therefore, to ensure the waste treatment capacity as well as power generation capac ity, the WtE 
plant shall be able to have a backup capacity. The installation of 3 turbines/generators with 30 
MW per unit has considered the back -up capacity for the closing of one turbine/generator to 
maintain or repair. In this case, the aim of the back -up design is to ensure that the two remaining 
turbines/generators  (60 MW) are still capable of generating up to 80% of the required capacity  
of 75 MW . According to the FSR which was designed by the third party and also approved the 
national authority, with  4,000 tons of MSW per day, the maximum electricity capacity of the plant 
is 75 MW. Moreover, based on the signed power purchase agreement (PPA) between the PO and 
EVN, the maximum electricity capacity exported to the national grid is 75 MW. The electricit y 
capacity will be capped at 75MW by both parties EVN and the project owner through the 
Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) system during operation. Therefore, the actual 
capacity of 75 MW is used in the financial analysis.  
The annual estimate d electricity output  of the project activity  of 388.05 GWh is supplied to the 
national grid via the transmission line. The Soc Son waste to power plant will use 5 Waterleau 
continuous grate -type incinerators for treating MSW and a combination of 5 boilers, 3 steam 
turbines and 3 generators to recover heat from combustion of MSW for generating electricity.

Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
5 
Scenario existing prior to the implementation of project activity:  
Prior to the implementation of the pro ject activity, MSW treatment in Viet Nam was mainly 
landfilling without any  methane recovery system for flaring or energy purpose, therefore methane 
emissions from anaerobic decay of waste would have been released into the atmosphere.  
MSW of Ha Noi is mai nly treated at Nam Son landfill site  in Nam Son waste treatment area and 
Xuan Son landfill site  in Xuan Son waste treatment area, which are operated by URENCO Ha Noi 
– a public entity. If there is no project activity, the MSW will continue to be disposed a t Nam Son 
landfill site. Nam Son landfill site was a sanitary landfill, operated in 2000. Nam Son solid waste 
treatment complex area  spans over 157 ha, its construction is divided into  2 phases. Phase 1 of 
83 ha contained 10 cells and was closed. Phase 2 o f more than 73 ha includes 8 cells and 
technical infrastructure items.  After many expansions and phases, up to now, this landfill 
receives about 5,000 tons of MSW/day, accounting for 77% of MSW generated by Ha Noi.  
According to Decision No.609/QD -TTg issued by the Prime Minister dated April 25, 2014, on 
Approving the master plan on solid waste disposal of Ha Noi capital to 2030, with a vision to 
2050, the Nam Son  landfill is planned to expand  its capacity  to 257 ha by 2030 and 280 ha by 
2050
1. Nam Son landfill was constructed with a depth of greater 9 m, and equipped with 
mechanical compacting, levelling of the waste, and cover material of soil 2. T here is one LFG 
recovery project (at closed cells in Nam Son landfill) that was implemented under Clean 
Development Mechanism (CDM) 3. The LFG ca pture system was however abandoned due to the 
low price of Certified Emission Reductions (CERs) in the market. 
For electricity component, prior to the implementation of the project activity, the electricity in Viet 
Nam was produced mainly from fossil fuel sources and solely distributed to customers through 
the unique national electricity grid managed by the Vietnam Electricity (EVN).  
Baseline Scenario:  
The baseline scenario of the project activity is the same as the scenario existing prior to the start 
of implementation of the project activity. The project activity is the installation of a Greenfield 
MSW treatment plant, the baseline scenario is disposal  of the fresh waste in a solid waste 
disposal site (SWDS) without an LFG capture. 
For electricity generation, the baseline scenario is electricity delivered to the grid by the project 
activity would have  otherwise been generated by the operation of grid -connected power plants 
and by the addition of  new generation sources.  
 
1 Decision No. 609/QD -TTg approving the Master Plan on solid waste disposal of Hanoi capital to 2030, with a vision to 
2050

Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
6 
Estimated GHG emission reductions:  
The project activity helps to avoid methane release from SWDS(s) in the absence of the project 
activity and generate renewable electricity, which displaced part of the electricity otherwise 
supplied by grid connected fossil fuel fired power plants. Thus, GHG emission reductions can be 
achieved. The expected CO
2 emission reduction from the proposed project is 544 ,012 tCO2e 
annually and 3, 808,082 tCO2e in total over the first 7 years crediting period . 
1.2 Sectoral Scope and Project Type 
Sectoral Scope : 1 - Energy industries (renewable / non -renewable sources) 
13 – Waste handling and disposal  
Project Category  : ACM0022 – Alternative waste treatment process - Version 03.0 
The proposed project is not a group project activity.  
1.3 Project Eligibility 
According to VCS Standard, version 4. 3, the scope of the VCS Program includes:  
1) The seven Kyoto Protocol greenhouse gases.  
2) Ozone-depleting substances (ODS).  
3) Project activities supported by a methodology approved under the VCS Program through 
the methodology  development and review  process.  
4) Project activities supported by a methodology approved under a VCS approved GHG 
program, unless explicitly excluded.  
5) Jurisdictional REDD+ programs and nested REDD+ projects as set out in the VCS Program 
document Jurisdictional and Nested REDD+ (JNR) Requirements.  
The project activity is to produce electricity from MSW that would have been left to decay 
anaerobically in waste landfills. The project will result in avoidance of quantity of methane (CH
4) 
that otherwise would have been released into the atmosphere due to the anaerobic decay of 
organic waste in landfills. Beside, the project will generate rene wable electricity, which displaced 
part of the electricity otherwise been supplied by grid connected fossil fuel fired power plants 
generating mainly CO
2 emission. Thus, GHG emission reductions can be achieved. This project 
type is not excluded from the Sc ope of VCS program as indicated in Table 1: Excluded project 
activities of VCS Standard, Version 4. 3. Therefore, the project activity is eligible to develop and 
register under the VCS Program . 
1.4 Project Design 
The project activity involves the construction a nd operation of Soc Son Waste to Power Plant with 
the MSW treatment capacity  of average 4,000 ton per day (equivalent to 1,460,000 tons per

Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
7 
year) and total electricity installed capacity of 90 MW (maximum electricity generation capacity 
to the national gir d of 75 MW). The main equipment of the project activity includes 5 Waterleau 
continuous grate -type incinerators for treating MSW (5 x 800 tons of MSW per day) and a 
combination of 5 boilers (5 x 65.86 ton of steam per hour), 3 steam turbines and 3 generators 
(3 x 30 MW).  
Eligibility Criteria 
The proposed project activity is a single installation activity, not a group project. Thus, this 
section is not applicable .

1.7 Ownership 
As per VCS Program Definitions, version 4. 2, the project ownership is the legal right to control 
and operate the project activit ies. 
Ha Noi Thien Y Environmental Energy Joint Stock Company is the project owner of Soc Son Waste 
to Power Plant Project. They have the legal right to control and operate and get any revenue from 
the project activity.    
The business license, project investment  license, MSW treatment contract, Power Purchase 
Agreement (PPA) and other documents as mentioned in Section 1.14 are evidences for the 
ownership of the plant, equipment and process that generates GHG emission reductions . 
Swiss Carbon Value Ltd. is a VCU purchaser generated from Soc Son Waste to Power Plant Project, 
which is demonstrated via Emission Reduction Purchase Agreement. Swiss Carbon Value Ltd is 
also responsible for investment and management of the project development under VCS . 
1.8 Project Start Date 
24/07/2022 (operation date of the proposed project activity) .  
1.9 Project Crediting Period 
This project adopts renewable crediting periods of 7 years which can be renewed for maximum 
2 times. 
The first crediting period is 7 years from 24/07/2022 to 23/07/2029 (both days included) .

1.11 Description of the Project Activity 
Prior to the implementation of the project activity, the same amount of MSW would be disposed 
at Nam Son landfill site. Nam Son landfill site was a sanitary landfill, operated in 2000. Currently, 
the landfill receives around 5,000 tons of MSW/day . As per Decision No.609 /QD-TTg on 
Approving the Master Plan on solid waste treatment of Hanoi to 2030, with a vision to 2050 
dated 25 April 2014, the  Nam Son  landfill is planned to  expand its capacity  to 6,000 tons per 
day by 2030 and 7,000 tons per day by 2050 (corresponding to an area of 257ha and 280ha by 
2030 and  2050, respectively) . The project activity is expected to use 4,000 tons of MS W/day. 
Therefore, the  Nam Son landfill area is sufficient  capacity to dispose waste with a comparable 
annual waste acceptance rate and with the same operating lifetime as the project activity.  
For electricity components, prior to the implementation of the project activity, the same amount 
of electricity would have been generated by the grid -connected power plants, which was  mainly 
from fossil fuel -based power plant.  The detailed information is mentioned in section 1.1 and 3.2 
on baseline scenario.  
The greenfield project activity involves the introduction  of the grate incineration technology to 
incinerate MSW at Hanoi city that would otherwise be disposed of at a local landfill site. The main 
equipment in the project activity includes 5 incinerators (800 ton of MSW per day), 5 boilers 
(65.8 ton per hour), 3 steam turbines and 3 generators (30 MW x 3 units), the transformer station 
and electricity transmission line, e xhaust gas cleaning system s and ash remove systems.   
After entering the treatment area through weighbridge s at the input gate, MSW will be moved to 
2 temporary waste bunkers, one is for  3 incineration lines, and another is for 2 remaining 
incineration lines. These tanks will be designed with maximum volume of 65,954 m 3 to store 
MSW within 8 -10 days. During storage in the waste bunkers, MSW will be turned and mixed to 
tear waste and remov e water to prevent anaerobic decomposition. Moreover, the air from the

Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
10 
waste bunkers will be blown to incinerator and used as a primary combustion air. MSW then 
provided to incinerators by clamshell buckets.  
Each moving grate incinerator is equipped a syst em, which feeding waste to grates, including a 
hopper, waste chute. Garbage is pushed into the combustion chamber by the system, then moved 
continuously and burned directly on the grate  in layer by layer, drying zone, burning zone and 
burn out zone. The un burnt components continue to move on the grate s to get out of the 
combustion chamber and fall into the slag collector  of furnace bottom. 
The heat recovered from  the incineration process will be provided to boilers. The superheated 
steam generated from the boiler s will be fed  into the steam turbine to generate kinetic energy . 
Turbines are connected to generators  to produce electricity. The electricity generated from the 
project activity will be exported to the national grid through the electricity transm ission line. 
The flue gases  from the incineration process including  NOx, SOx, HCl, heavy metal, dioxin, furan 
will be treated by the  exhausted gases control technology. This includes combustion control, 
including activated carbon adsorption, semi -dry deacidification, baghouse filter . After the treated 
clean gas, which meet s Viet Nam Environmental Quality Standard  will be release to the 
atmosphere from chimneys . The slag will be collected and transported to brick production plant. 
While fly ash will be solidified , then sent to the landfill site.    
The leachate generated from MSW bunkers will  be collected and gone  into the wastewater 
treatment system and is treated together with other wastewater sources by  the UASB technology 
to meet Viet Nam Environmental Quality Standard  before discharging the environment.

1.13 Conditions Prior to Project Initiation 
The project is a Greenfield project and does not involve generation of GHG  
emissions for the purpose of their subsequent reduction, removal or destruction. Thus p rior to 
project initiation, there was nothing at site.  
Project activity

The baseline scenario is the same as the conditions existing prior to the project initiation; 
please refer to section 2.4 for further information .  
1.14 Compliance with Laws, Statutes and Other Regulatory Frameworks 
The project Owner has received necessary approvals for development and commission ing of the 
project from the State agencies and is in compliance to the local laws and regulations. This has 
been demonstrated through below supporting do cuments: 
- Business license No. 0108186977 for Ha Noi Thien Y Environmental Energy Joint Stock 
Company issued by Investment and Planning Department of Hanoi dated on 
03/03/2020, replaced from first registration dated 15/03/2018. It is compliance with 
the Law on enterprise No. 68/2014/QH13.  
- Investment license No. 7698780162 issued by Investment and Planning Department of 
Hanoi dated on 15/07/2020 on certifying Ha Noi Thien Y Environmental Energy JSC is a 
project owner of Soc Son Waste to Power Plant, replaced from the first license dated 
29/07/2017. It is compliance with Law on Investment No. 67/2014 /QH13. 
- Official Letter No. 1281/ TTg -CN issued by the Prime Minister dated 20/09/2018 on 
additional approval of Soc Son Waste to Power plant project into  the Adjusted Power 
Development Planning VII. It is compliance with the Electricity Law and the Circula r No. 
43/2013/QH13 dated 31/12/2013 issued by Ministry of Industry and Trade, regulations 
on content, sequence, procedures for formulation, assessment, approval and adjustment 
to electricity development planning.  
- Official Letter No. 207/HDXD -QLDA dated 10 /04/2019 and No. 367/HDXD -QLDA dated 
02/07/2020 issued by Department of Construction Management on approval of the 
Feasibility Study Report and Adjusted FSR of the project respectively. It is compliance with 
Law on Investment No. 67/2014/QH13. 
- Decision No.  1324/QD-BTNMT dated 28/05/2019 issued by Ministry of Natural 
Resources and Environment (MONRE)  on approval of Environmental Impact Assessment 
Report (EIA report) for the project activity. It is compliance with Law on Environmental 
Protection No. 55/2014/QH13. 
- Land use right certificate issued by Hanoi People’s Committee dated 11/07/2019 for Ha 
Noi Thien Y Environmental Energy JSC to construct and operate the plant. It is compliance 
with Land Law No.  45/2013/QH13.  
- Power Purchase Agreement (PPA) No. 05/2019 /HD-NMDR-Soc Son dated 23/05/2019 
between the Vietnam Electricity and Ha Noi Thien Y Environmental Energy JSC. It is 
compliance with the Electricity Law.  
- Municipal Solid Waste Treatment Contract No. 08/2018/HĐVXLCTRSH dated 
09/08/2018 between Ha Noi Thien Y Environmental Energy JSC and People’s Committee 
of Hanoi city. It is compliance with the Environmental Protection Law . 
1.15 Participation under Other GHG Programs 
 Projects Registered (or seeking registration) under Other GHG Program(s) 
The proposed project activity has neither been registered nor seeking registration under any other 
GHG programs.

Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
15 
The proposed project activity is seeking registration only in VCS program . 
 Projects Rejected by Other GHG Programs 
The proposed project activity is not participating in other environment credits, other GHG 
programs and has not been rejected by any other GHG Programs . 
1.16 Other Forms of Credit 
 Emissions Trading Programs and Other Binding Limits 
GHG emissions reductions generated from the proposed project activ ity will not be used for 
compliance with any other emissions trading program or to meet other binding limits on GHG 
emissions other than VCS Program .. 
 Other Forms of Environmental Credit 
The project has not sought or received another form of environmental credits, including 
renewable energy certificates . 
1.17 Sustainable Development Contributions 
The project contributes to the sustainable development on the local, regional, national scale in 
many aspects of environment, Sociality and Economy, as follows:  
Environmental benefits:  
• Instead of anaerobic decay of biodegradation waste that would have occurred in landfills, 
resulting in methane generation. The project activity will contribute to mitigation of 
greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions through incineration of the waste. The annual av erage 
expected emission reduction from the project activity is 544,012 tCO2e, which contributes 
to SDG  13 “Respond in a timely and effective manner to climate change and natural 
disaster” as per mentioned in Resolution on sustainable  development No 136/NQ -CP 
dated 25/09/2020 of The Viet nam Government. Emission reduction will be calculated at 
each monitoring period.  
• The Project activity would reduce the amount of waste going into the dumping site thus 
increasing the lifetime of the waste landfill sites. The purpose of this project activity is to 
treat average 4,000 ton of municipal solid waste (MSW) per day. Total annual amount of 
municipal sol id waste treated is estimated 1,460,000 ton , increasing quantity of waste 
at city treated by modern technology, preventing from disposing at landfill sites, which 
contributes to  Goal of SDG 11 “Promote sustainable and resilient urban and rural 
development; ensure safe living and working environments; ensure reasonable 
distribution of population and work force by region” as per mentioned in Resolution on 
sustainable development No 136/NQ -CP dated 25/09/2020 of The Viet nam 
Government. The quantity of municip al waste treated will be measured continuously by 
weighbridge and gathered at each monitoring period.

Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
16 
• Contributing to prevent the soil, air and water pollution and improve Environment quality 
for local residents.  
• Through recovering heat from incineration a nd generating clean power, the project also 
contributes to mitigate exhausted gases (GHG included), which would otherwise have 
been released into atmosphere because of production of electricity from fossil-fuel power 
plants. 
Economic benefits:  
• During the o peration phase, the project activity is estimated to supply 388.05 GWh  of 
renewable energy to the national grid annual, which contributes to SDG 7 “Ensure access 
to sustainable, reliable and affordable energy for all” as per mentioned in Resolution on 
sustainable development No 136/NQ -CP dated 25/09/2020 of The Viet nam 
Government.  
• Advantage of incineration is that treat MSW thoroughly, minimize volume of waste, save 
land area to use other economic purposes instead of MSW landfill treatment.  
Social benefits : 
• Introducing the advanced waste to power technology on solid waste treatment that is not 
yet commonly applied in Viet Nam.  
• Creating jobs during the construction and the operation phase and contribute to 
improving income for people. During the operation phase, the project activity is estimated 
to create approximately 180 jobs, which contributes  to SDG  8 “ Ensure sustained, 
inclusive and sustainable economic growth; full and productive employment and decent 
work for all” as per mentioned in Resolution on sustainable development No 136/NQ -CP 
dated 25/09/2020 of The Viet nam Government. List of employees at the plant will be 
monitored and provided at each monitoring period.  
• Improving the technical skills of workers via trainings to operate the w aste treatment 
plant.  
1.18 Additional Information Relevant to the Project  
Leakage Management 
According to the applied methodology of ACM0022, version 0 3.0, no leakage is considered for 
incineration of MSW.  
Commercially Sensitive Information  
No commercially s ensitive information has been excluded from the public version of the project 
description. 
Further Information 
No further information is required.

Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
17 
2 SAFEGUARDS 
2.1 No Net Harm 
The “Environmental Impact Assessment Report” (EIAR) on 04/2019 of the project activity has 
been approved by MONRE via issuing Decision No. 1324/ QD -BTNMT on the Approval of EIAR 
dated 28/05/2019 for the Soc Son waste to power plant.  
All potential negative impacts in construction and operation phase will be strictly applied 
mitigation measures to minimi ze the effect to the environment. Detail information is  described 
further in section 2.3  below. 
Therefore, there will be  no harm identi fied from the proposed project activity . 
2.2 Local Stakeholder Consultation 
During implementation process, project owner conducted two  meetings for local stakeholder 
consultation on socioeconomic and environmental impact assessment.  
- The first LSC meeting fo r the proposed project activity was organized on 20/0 6/2018 at  
Communal Hall, Nam Son  commune, Soc Son  district, Ha Noi.  Before the meeting was taken 
place, representative of local authority, impacted local people were informed and invited to the 
meeting through public speaker, personal invitations dated 14/06/2018.  
The participants of the meeting include as follows:  
• Local authorities: Representative of Nam Son Commune People’s Committee; 
representative of Village s of commune. 
• Representative of the project participants  
• Communities: 37 people including farmers (local people) ; local NGO such as Youth 
Union, Farmer Association, Fatherland Front, Woman’s Union.   
- The second meeting was organized on 04/10/2019 at Communal Hall, Nam Son commune, 
Soc Son district, Ha Noi. The invitation letters also were sent to representative of local authority, 
impacted local people  one week before the meeting was taken place.  There are 15 participants 
attended the meeting.

The mechanism for on -going communication with local stakeholders : 
The mechanisms for on -going communication with local stakeholders include phone, mail and 
direct inquiries locally with the project’s managers at the project’s site, as well as Nam Son  
commune grievance submissions.

Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
19 
- Project site is the place where the local stakeholders can communicate directly (or 
anonymously via the comment book) with the projec t owner. The office of the communal 
People’s Committee is a standing unit of the People’s Committee to deal with comments 
from local community on all matters of the commune and is the contact point between 
local authority and the residents. The comment boo ks will be securely placed in the 
chosen locations and daily checked by responsible persons. Administrative Department 
of PO will receive inputs or comments from stakeholders and forward to relevant 
department to corporately address and close issues.  
- The telephones are located at the office of the project owner at the project’s site and at 
the office of the VCS consultancy company to allow more practical communication with 
local stakeholders. There is always a receptionist on the desk to answer the calls or  have 
the messages recorded. All received calls shall be logged and recorded in Comment Book 
with the date, comments, action requested and project responses. Stakeholders are not 
required to give their personal details when they wish to make a comment.  
- The notification on continuous grievance mechanism is also public on t he website of 
consultant unit . This  is always available to get any comment from stakeholders.

2.3 Environmental Impact 
Pursuant to the Law on Environmental Protection No. 55/2014/QH13 dated 23/06/2014, 
Decree No.18/2015/ND -CP dated 14/02/2015 on Environmental protection planning, strategy 
environmental assessment (SEA), environmental impact assessme nt (EIA) and environmental 
protection plans, the EIA has been carried out Apr 2019 and already approved by MONRE  via 
Decision No. 1324/ QD -BTNMT dated 28/05/2019 . 
As per the EIAR , the possible environmental impacts of the project and responding 
measurements taken by the project owner are summarized as follows: 
Impacts and mitigation measures in the co nstruction phase : 
Dust and exhaust gases, noise  
Dust, exhaust gases and noise generated from vehicles for transportation of materials, 
machines, equipment and construction equipment, or ground levelling activities. However, these 
emissions are short term and localized at the project area. The PO also co nducts mitigation 
measures to minimize any potential native impacts as follow:  
- Request contractors to implement environmental protection measures as well as 
supervise their compliance.  
- Using advanced  vehicles, machines and equipment must meet  Viet Nam Sta ndard on 
technical and environmental safety. They also have to be maintained periodically.

Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
20 
- Don’t transport excess regulated weight; covering materials on transportation equipment  
when incoming or going out of the project site.  
- Arranging operation time of  vehicles and construct ion suitably, avoiding gather materials 
at the same time. Avoid construction activities during peak -hours. 
- Covering and watering construction areas, to prevent from dispersion of dust into  the 
ambient environment. 
- Provide labour prot ection equipment such as safety mask s, sa fety helmets, protective 
clothes for workers. 
Wastewater 
Domestic wastewater:  
Domestic wastewater comes  from workers’ activities. To mitigate the impacts of wastewater, the 
following measures will be conducted by the PO:  
- Increase using of local workers, install portable composite toilets. During the 
construction phase, the contractors will hire functional units to collect and treat waste 
from storage tanks of portable toilets.  
- Rainfall flow in construction site, it can contain excavated soil or waste, then it will be 
preliminarily deposited at  Pits before coming out the environment.  
- Prohibit washing equipment, machines at water sources or places which washing water 
can directly flow into the drainage system of the area.  
Construction wastewater:  
Construction wastewater from washing area, mixing materials mainly contains suspended dust 
and may fal l into and make blockage the general drainage system. Therefore, mitigation 
measures will be implemented to prevent from this:  
- Construction wastewater will be led to pits before flow into the general drainage system.  
- Don’t gather materials near the drainage system.  
- Always check and dredging periodically if construction wastes fall into the drainage 
system. 
Solid waste 
The solid waste will be collected and treated by URENCO during the construction phase.  
Impacts and mitigation measures in the operation phase: 
Gases emission  
Gases emission from  the transportation of MSW, incinerator s or wastewater treatment system . 
These emissions will be managed and handled with the following measures: 
- Trucks loading MSW must be covered to avoid dropping or emitting the bad odor of MSW.

Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
21 
- After moving MSW to waste bunkers, trucks will be washed before coming out  of the 
plant. 
- Periodically spaying deodorant chemical to minimize bad odor, ba cteria. 
- Plant trees around the plant to improve  the microclimate. 
- For flue gas, they will be treated in gas emission treatment system including  the De-NOx 
system (SNCR - Selective non-Catalytic Reduction), acid removal tower, dioxin and heavy 
metal removal by activated carbon, bag filters to extract dust from gas st ream and 
chimney system. The c ontinuous monitoring system will be installed to monitor main 
pollutants in flue gas (NO 2, SO2, CO, HCl and dust) and treated gases emission need to 
meet the environmental quality standard before emitting the atmosphere. 
- Fly ash is solidified and landfilled while bottom ash is transported to  the brick production 
facility. 
Wastewater 
The w astewater from domestic activities (handled in septic tanks), leachates, washing of 
workshop and equipment will be led to  the leachate treatment system and treated to meet the 
environmental quality standard before discharging receiver sources.  
Solid waste  
The solid waste will be gathered and moved to  the waste reception area to  be used as input 
waste of the plant .

This project was open for public comment from 11/08/2021 to 10/09/2021. No comments were 
received during the public comment period.  11 
2.5 AFOLU-Specific Safeguards 
The project activity involves to construct a  MSW treatment plant, a non-AFOLU project, thus this

3.3 Project Boundary 
As per methodology ACM0022, version 0 3.0, the spatial extent of the project activity is the SWDS 
where the waste is disposed of in the baseline, anaerobic lagoons or sludge pits treating orga nic 
wastewater in the baseline , and  the site of the alternative waste treatment process (es). The 
boundary also includes on -site electricity and/or heat generation and use, on -site fuel use and 
the wastewater treatment plant used to treat the wastewater by-products of the alternative waste 
treatment process(es). The project boundary does not include facilities for waste collection and 
transport.  
In the case that the project provides electricity to a grid, then the spatial extent of the project 
boundary wil l also include those plants connected to the energy system to which the plant is 
connected.

does not claim the emission reduction from anaerobic lagoons or sludge pits treating organic 
wastewater. Then the project boundary will include the SWDS where the waste is disposed of in 
the baseline , the site of treatment process,  on-site electricity and/or heat generation and use, 
on-site fuel use, the wastewater treatment plant, and those plants connected to the national grid.

3.4 Baseline Scenario 
According to methodology ACM0022, version 2.0, identify the baseline scenario and demonstrate 
additionality using the “Combined tool to identify the baseline scenario and demonstrate 
additionality” and following the requirements below .  
Step 0: Demonstration whether the proposed project activity is the first -of its-kind 
This step is not applied to the project activity since it is not first-of-its-kind, hence the additionality 
of the project will be demonstrated in next steps below.  
Step 1: Identification of alternative scenarios  
Sub-step 1a: Define alternative scenarios to the proposed project activity:  
Identify all altern ative scenarios that provide the same output (service or product) as the 
proposed project activity. These alternative scenarios shall include:  
(a) M1: The project activity without being registered as a VCS project activity (i.e. any 
(combination) of the waste treat ment options listed in Table 2 of methodology);  
(b) M2: Disposal of the fresh waste in a SWDS with a partial capture of the LFG and flaring of 
the captured LFG;   
(c) M3: Disposal of the fresh waste in a SWDS without a LFG capture system;  
(d) M4: Part of the fresh fraction of the solid waste is recycled and not disposed in the SWDS;  
(e) M5: Part of the fresh fraction of the solid waste is treated aerobically and not disposed in the 
SWDS;  
(f) M6: Part of the organic fraction of the solid waste is incinerated and not disposed  in the 
SWDS; 
(g) M7: Part of the organic fraction of the solid waste is gasified and not disposed in the SWDS;  
(h) M8: Part of the organic fraction of the solid waste is treated in an anaerobic digester and not 
disposed in the SWDS;  
(i) M9: Part of the organic fract ion of the solid waste is mechanically or thermally treated to 
produce RDF/SB and not disposed in the SWDS.

Sub-step 1b: Consistency with mandatory laws and regulations:  
For component of solid waste treatment , legal framework on waste management was developed 
in Viet Nam with relevant laws and regulations as follows : 
• Law on Environmental Protection No 72/2020/QH14, dated on 17/11/2020. 
• Decision No. 491/QD -TTg dated on 07/05/2018 of the Prime Minister on approving 
adjustments to national strategy for general management of solid  waste to 2025 with 
vision towards 2050 
• Decree No. 38/2015/ ND -CP dated 24/04/2015 of the Government on management of 
waste and scraps 
Decree No. 38/2015/ ND-CP states that the solid waste treatment technology shall be chosen 
in accordance with available technology feasibility and local conditions. The solid waste 
treatment technologies can be selected such as composting; incineration; sanitary landfill ; 
technology of recycle, energy recovery, product  producing; advanced and other environmentally -
friendly technologies.  Therefore, all above alternatives (M1, M3) are consistent with mandatory 
laws and regulations in Viet Nam.

37 
For component of electricity generation, the alternative P1 is consistent with mandatory laws and 
regulations of Viet Nam as it was issued the Investment Certificate by People’s Committee of 
Hanoi. While P6 is a business -as- usual scenario in electricity generation.  
Step 2: Barrier analys is 
Step 2a: Identify barriers that would prevent the implementation of alternative scenarios  
For the Option 1 (M1 + P1), no barrier other than insufficient financial returns as analyzed  in 
Step 3 is identified. In terms of Combination Option  2 (M3+P6), it doesn’t face a ny barrier as it 
is the current practice . 
Step 2b: Eliminate alternative scenarios which are prevented by the identified barriers  
From step 2a, there is no barrier other t han insufficient financial returns as analyzed in Step 3 . 
And there is two remaining alternatives to the project activity, which is outcome of step 2a, thus 
the proposed project activity is not the first -of-its-kind, then proceed to Step 3.  
Step 3: Investment analysis 
Determine whether the proposed project activity is not:  
(a) The most economically or financially attractive; or  
(b) Economically or financially feasible, without the revenue from the sale of emission 
reductions 
To conduct the investment analysis, the following sub-steps are applied:  
Sub-step 3a: Determine appropriate analysis method  
Tool07 – “Tool for the demonstration and assessment of additionality”, Version 7.0.0 provides 
three methods of investment analysis: simple cost analysis (Option I), investm ent comparison 
analysis (Option II) and benchmark analysis (Option III).  
The proposed project activity generates financial and economic benefits from treatment of MSW 
and sale of the generated electricity other than VCU revenues, so the simple cost analysis (Option 
I) is not applicable. Out of the two remaining options, Option II is also not applicable as there are 
no other credible and realistic baseline scenario alternatives other than  Combination of Disposal 
of fresh waste in a landfill without a LFG capture system and Electricity supply from the grid.   
Thus, the benchmark analysis (Option III) is chosen to prove additionality.  
Sub-step 3b (Option III): Apply benchmark analysis  
The inv estment analysis using Benchmark analysis approach (Option III) has been chosen.  
In the following, Project IRR is used to demonstrate the additionality of the project.  
As indicated in paragraph 15, Tool27 – “Investment analysis”, Version 1 1.0, “Local commercial 
lending rates or weighted average costs of capital (WACC) are appropriate benchmarks for a 
project IRR”, the project participant applies the local commercial lending rates as a benchmark

The benchmark is 11% at the date of making the investment decision on 16 December 2018.

Step (2): Identify similar projects (both CDM/VCS and non- CDM/VCS) which fulfil all of the 
following conditions:  
a) The projects are located in the applicable geographical area; 
b) The projects apply the same measure as the proposed project activity; 
c) The projects use the same energy source/fuel and feedstock as the proposed project 
activity, if a technology switch measure is implemented by the proposed project activity; 
d) The plants in which the projects are  implemented produce goods or services with 
comparable quality, properties and applications areas (e.g. clinker) as the proposed project 
plant;  
e) The capacity or output of the projects is within the applicable capacity or output range 
calculated in Step 1; 
f) The projects started commercial operation before the project design document (CDM-PDD) 
is published for global stakeholder consultation or before the start date of proposed project 
activity whichever is earlier for the proposed project activity.  
Identification of the similar projects (CDM/VCS and non-CDM/VCS) is carried out as per sub-steps 
of Step (2) as follows: 
a) The proposed project is located in Viet Nam so the entire host country (Viet  Nam) was 
chosen as the applicable geographical area. 
b) The proposed project activity is a Greenfield waste to power plant. Therefore, all the MSW 
treatment plants and generating electricity (the same technology  as the proposed project 
activity) are candidates for similar projects. 
c) The energy source used by the proposed project activity is MSW. Hence, only waste to 
power plants are candidates for similar projects.  
d) The propose project activity produces electricity from MSW . Therefore, all the waste to  
power plants are candidates for similar projects 
e) The capacity range of the similar projects is within the applicable capacity range from 
2,000 tons/day to 6,000 tons/day. 
f) Since CDM Tools/Guidelines are followed for common practice analysis, the start date 
needs to be considered as per CDM guidelines for common practice analysis. The start 
date of the project under the Methodological tool for common practice is the date on 
which the project participants commit to making expenditures for the construction or 
modification of the main equipment or facility (e.g. a wind turbine), or for the provision or 
modification of a service (e.g. distribution of energy -efficient light bulbs, change of 
transport management system). Where a contract is signed for such expenditures ( e.g. 
for procurement of a wind turbine), it is the date on which the contract is signed . In other 
cases, it is the date on which such expenditures are incurred. If the project activity 
involves more than one of such contracts or incurred expenditures, it i s the first of the 
respective dates. Activities incurring minor pre -project expenses (e.g. feasibility studies, 
preliminary surveys) are not considered in the determination of the start date.  
The equipment purchase contract between Ha Noi Thien Y Environmental Energy JSC and Jiangsu 
Tianying Environmental Energy Complete Equipment Co., Ltd  was signed on 18/12/2018 , is the 
first contract for expenditures of the proposed project activity and it is considered as the start 
date of the project activity for common practice analysis. The waste to power projects, which have 
started commercial operation before 18/ 12/2018 have been considered for analysis.

As per version 07.0 of "Tool to calculate the emission factor for an electricity system", following 
steps are applied in the calculation of the emission factor for the baseline scienario:  
Step 1: Identify the relevant electricity systems;  
Step 2: Choose whether to include off -grid power plants in the project electricity system 
(optional); 
Step 3: Select a method to determine the operating margin (OM);  
Step 4: Calculate the operating margin emission factor according to the selected 
method; 
Step 5: Calculate the build margin (BM) emission factor;

Step 1. Identify the relevant electricity systems  
This project will be connected to the national electricity grid of Vietnam, which is operated and 
monopolized by the EVN. This national electricity grid is the unique transmission and distribution 
line, to which all power plants in Vietnam are physically connected. Hence the national electricity 
grid is the project electricity system.  
There are electricity imports to the national electricity grid from China - another host country, 
thus the China Power Grid is the connected electricity system and the emission factor for the 
imported electricity is zero tons CO
2 per MWh by default . 
Step 2. Choose whether to include off -grid power plants in the project electricity system 
(optional) 
There are two options to calculate the operating margin and build margin emission factor:  
Option 1: Only grid power plants are included in the calculation; 
Option 2: Both grid power plants and off -grid power plants are included in the calculation  
Because only the data of grid connected power plants is available, so Option I will be chosen for 
calculating the grid emission factor.  
Step 3. Select a method to determine the operating margin (OM);  
The calculation of the operating margin emission factor (EF grid,OM,y ) is based on one of the 
following methods:  
a) Simple OM; or 
b) Simple adjusted OM; or  
c) Dispatch data analysis OM; or  
d) Average OM. 
The method (a) “Simple OM” can be chosen in Vietnam because total electricity generation of 
low cost/must-run resources is less than 50% of total grid generation in average of the fice 
most recent years (detail see in the table below):

Step 4. Calculate the OM emission factor according to the selected method  
The simple OM emission factor is calculated as the generation -weighted average CO2 emissions 
per unit net electricity generation (tCO 2/MWh) of all generating power plants serving the 
system, not including low -cost/must-run power plants units.  
Simple OM may be calculated by one of the following two options:  
Option A: Based on the net electricity generation and a CO2 emission factor of  
each power unit; or  
Option B: Based on the total net electricity generation of all power plants serving 
the system and the fuel types and total fuel consumption of the project 
electricity system  
Because the necessary data for Option A is available so Option A “Calculation based on average 
efficiency and electricity generation of each plant” is used and then the simple OM emission 
factor is calculated as follows:

Step 5. Calculate the BM emission factor  
In terms of vintage of data, one of the following two options can be chosen:  
Option 1 - For the first crediting period, calculate the build margin emission factor ex ante 
based on the most recent information available on units already built for sample group m at the 
time of VCS-PDD submission to the DOE for validation. For the second crediti ng period, the build 
margin emission factor should be updated based on the most recent information available on 
units already built at the time of submission of the request for renewal of the crediting period to 
the DOE. For the third crediting period, the  build margin emission factor calculated for the 
second crediting period should be used. This option does not require monitoring the emission 
factor during the crediting period, or  
Option 2 - For the first crediting period, the build margin emission factor  shall be updated 
annually, ex post, including those units built up to the year of registration of the project activity 
or, if information up to the year of registration is not yet available, including those units built up 
to the latest year for which info rmation is available. For the second crediting period, the build 
margin emissions factor shall be calculated ex ante, as described in Option 1 above. For the 
third crediting period, the build margin emission factor calculated for the second crediting 
period should be used.

Step 6. Calculate the CM emission factor  
According to the tool, the calculation of the combined margin (CM) emission factor (EFgrid, 
CM,y) is based on one of the following methods:  
(a) Weighted average CM; or  
(b) Simplified CM  
As the project is located in Vietnam a developing country and having more than 10 registered 
projects at starting da te of validation, the PP chooses the weighted average CM method to 
calculate CM emission factor for the proposed project.  
The combined margin emissions factor is calculated as follows:  
EFgrid,CM,y = EFgrid,OM,y × wOM + EFgrid,BM,y × wBM 
Where: 
EFgrid,CM,y = Combined margin CO 2 emission factor of national grid in year y  
(tCO2/MWh) 
EFgrid,OM,y = Operating margin CO 2 emission factor of national grid in year y 
(tCO2/MWh) 
wOM = Weighting of operating margin emissions factor  (%) 
EFgrid,BM,y = Build margin CO 2 emission factor of national grid in year y 
(tCO2/MWh) 
wBM = Weighting of build margin emissions factor  (%) 
The DNA report applied the weighting factors of both OM and  BM emission are 0.5, which is  
applicable for the project activity in the first crediting period and subsequent crediting periods.   
So the CM emissions factor is derived as follows:

4.3 Leakage 
As per methodology ACM0022, version 03 .0, leakage emissions are associated with 
composting/co-composting, anaerobic digestion and the use of RDF/SB that is exported outside 
the project boundary. Since the project activity uses incineration technology to treat MSW, 
leakage emissions are not considered. Therefore, LE y = 0. 
4.4 Net GHG Emission Reductions and Removals 
Reductions are calculated as follows:  
ERy = BEy – PEy – LEy [Equation 36 of ACM0022]  
Where: 
ERy = Emission reductions in year y (t CO 2e/yr)

Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
65 
BEy = Baseline emissions in year y (t CO 2e/yr) 
PEy = Project emissions in year y (t CO 2e/yr) 
LEy = Leakage emission in year y (t CO2e/yr)

If the sum of PE y and LE y is smaller than 1 per cent of BE y in the first full operation year of a 
crediting period, the project participants may choose to assume a fixed percentage of 1 per cent 
for the sum of PE y and LEy for the remaining years of the crediting period.  
In the case that overall negative emission reductions arise in a year, CERs are not issued to 
project participants for the year concerned and in subsequent years, until emission reductions 
from subsequent years have compensated the quantity of negative emission redu ctions from 
the year concerned .

Use the following values for MCF: 
(a) 1.0 for anaerobic managed solid waste disposal sites.  
These must have controlled placement of waste (i.e., 
waste directed to specific deposition areas, a degree of 
control of scavenging and a degree of control of fires) and 
will include at least one of the following: (i) cover material;

Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
73 
(ii) mechanical compacting; or (iii) levelling of the waste. 
(b) 0.5 for semi-aerobic managed solid waste disposal sites. 
These must have controlled placement of waste and will 
include all of the following structures for introducing air to 
waste layer: (i) permeable cover material; (ii) leachate 
drainage system; (iii) regulating pondage; and (iv) gas 
ventilation system. 
(c) 0.8 for unmanaged solid waste disposal sites – deep 
and/or with high water table. This comprises all SWDS not 
meeting the criteria of managed SWDS and which have 
depths of greater than or equal to 5 meters and/or high 
water table at near ground level. Latter situation 
corresponds to filling inland water, such as pond, river or 
wetland, by waste. 
(d)  0.4 for unmanaged-shallow solid waste disposal sites. 
This comprises all SWDS not meeting the criteria of 
managed SWDS and which have depths of less than 5 
metres. 
As mentioned in section 1.1, t he baseline landfill is a n anaerobic 
managed landfill with a depth of greater 9 meter, and equipped 
with mechanical compacting, levelling of the waste, and cover 
material of soil .  Therefore, the value of 1.0 is used .

5.3 Monitoring Plan 
The monitoring plan is developed in accordance with the procedures for VCS project activity and 
for the project being implemented. The monitoring plan, which will be implemented by the project 
participant describes about the monitoring equipment, monitoring organizational structure, policies 
for oversight and responsibility of personnel, monitoring procedure, parameters to be monitored,

Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
88 
calibration of monitoring equipment, quality assurance/ quality control procedures, data stor age 
and archiving procedures, emergency procedures, training. 
A. Monitoring equipment 
The main monitoring equipment in the plant will be as follow: 
• Weighbridges including four weighbridges with capacity of 50 tons/time and one weigh bridge 
with capacity of 80 tons/time will be installed at the entrance of the plant. The weight bridge 
will record the weight of each waste truck going in the plant. Data will be recorded 
continuously, aggregated daily and monthly. The project owner takes responsibili ty for the 
operation and maintenance of the weight bridge in accordance with manufacturer’s technical 
specifications and requirements.  
• Wastewater flow meters will be installed at before wastewater treatment system to measure 
amount of wastewater treated anaerobically from the project activity . 
• The electricity delivered to and imported from the national grid will be measured by power 
meters.  
Power meters are installed at the connection point at bus -bar 110 kV of 220 kV Soc Son 
transformer station.  The installed electricity meters are digital bi -direction meters with 
allowed errors of “±0.2s” for main meter and “±0.5s” for backup meters. Electricity metering 
system includes main metering system and  two backup metering systems are installed 
according to "Technical design agreement of power metering system, data collection and 
measurement system of Soc Son Waste to power plant”, No.1927/EPTC -KT&CNTT-KDBD 
dated 09/05/2019 between EVN and the Project Owner in accordance with the electrical 
metering regulations.  
Before the power metering equipment puts into operation, the PO and EVN shall check and 
accept it. Each terminal block of these equipment is sealed with lead to prevent all the 
unallowable interferences.  
The power meter type used is the electronic  3 phases: 
Item Main meter 1st backup meters 2nd backup meters 
Type Three phases, 4 wires  Three phases, 4 wires  Three phases, 4 wires  
Model Elster – A1700 Elster – A1700 Elster – A1700 
Manufacturer UK UK UK 
Accuracy 0.2s 0.5s 0.5s 
 
B. Organizational structure, responsibilities of personnel, policies for oversight and accountability of 
monitoring activities 
The Project Owner has established a special VCS group to take charge of data collection, 
supervision, recording and data storage. VCS group is trained and get technical support by the VCS 
consultation organization.  
The structure of monitoring group and the responsibility of members in the VCS Group are the 
following:

Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
89 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Figure 5: Structure of the monitoring group  
The responsibilities of each person involved are elaborated as follows: 
Table 14. Group members and their responsibilities  
Person Responsibility 
Representative of the

Review the monitoring report annually.

project, guiding and supervising data recorder after trained by VCS 
consultant. 
VCS consultant Providing trainings and technical support about VCS monitoring plan.  
Data recorder Collecting and recording data every month.  
Equipment 
supervisor  
Checking equipment periodically according to relevant regulation.  
Internal auditor Checking the monitoring procedures, double checking the collected 
data. 
 
C. Data collection procedure 
Data collection procedure of main parameters are well integrated into MSW management 
procedure of People’s Committee of Ha Noi for amount of input MSW and the standard EVN 
procedure for electricity generated.  
The amount of waste to be incinerated will be measured and recorded automatically when each 
waste truck enters the incineration plant through the truck scale installed at the gate . The data 
will be aggregated monthly, and based on the record to calculate the tipping fee. The MSW 
treatment income recei pt will be provided for double check.  
The proportion of different type of waste will be determined randomly a sample taken during one 
month. The sample taken from the waste bunker is classified into (1) Paper/ cardboard; (2) 
Textile; (3) Food waste; (4) W ood; (5) Garden and Park waste; (6) Nappies; (7) Rubber and 
Leather; (8) Plastics; (9) Metal  and glass; (10) O thers. The mass as well as the percentage of 
each portion will be determined and recorded manually .  
The electricity generated and imported from the power grid will be measured continuously by two 
way power meters installed at the substation. M onthly, EVN staff and staff of the operation 
Representative of the Company  
VCS group manager 
Equipment supervisor 
Data recorder 
 Internal auditor 
VCS consultant

Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
90 
division of the plant will cross -check manual readings from the power meter with the 
electronically recorded data  and sign a joint balance sheet which indicates the amount of power 
fed into the grid within that month and electricity imported from the grid. This joint balance sheet 
is also the basis of payment between the EVN and the project proponent. The PO provides  
electricity sales invoices to EVN as well as receive electricity purchases invoices from EVN.  
The type and quantity of auxiliary fuel consumption will be monitored through the provision of 
fuel purchase invoices.  
Other monitored data will be measured wi th frequency of monitoring as described in section 5.2. 
Then, data will be collected and recorded monthly by recorders and reported to VCS group 
manager and provide to VVB during each given verification.   
D.  Data archiving procedure 
- The VCS group appointed by the plant  shall keep monitored data in electronic archives 
at the end of every month. Paper documents should be stored in electronic format.  
- All the data and information in the form of paper documents shall be archived by the VC S 
group, with at least one copy backup for each datum.  
- Data monitored and required for verification and issuance are to be kept for two years 
after the end of the crediting period or the last issuance of VCUs for this project activity, 
whichever occurs later. 
E. The procedures for handling non -conformances with the validated monitoring plan   
In case of any unforeseen event that is not covered under this monitoring plan, staff of the 
operation division will immediately inform the chief of the operation divi sion. The chief of the 
operation division is then responsible to ensure that the cause for the unforeseen event is 
detected, the event is remedied and for the period of time in which the unforeseen event has 
occurred uncertainty in data gathered is limited  as much as possible. In doing so, the chief of 
division shall seek guidance from VCS consultant. 
If the weighbridges are broken, the amount of input solid waste will be adopted as minimum  
historical data for resulting minimum baseline emissions. H owever, this case will be rarely 
happened due to 5 weighbridges working in shifts, the case of one broken, the others will work full 
capacity continuously
. 
If the wastewater flow meter is broken, the amount of wastewater will be historical data for a 
maximum project emission. 
The main and backup power meters are used in order to record the electricity generated to the grid.  
- In normal condition, the data of main power meter will be used as the basis of payment 
by the EVN to the PO and to calculate the emission  reductions by the project activity.  
- In case the main power meter is in failure and the backup power meter is still in good 
operation, the result of the backup meter will be used to calculate the emission reduction 
by the project activity. 
- In case of both main and backup power meters are in failure, the Project Owner and the 
power company (EVN) will jointly calculate a conservative estimate of power supplied to 
the grid. The assumptions used to estimate net electricity supply to the grid will be signed 
by both a representative of the project owner as well as a representative of the power 
company (EVN). 
- In case any power meters are in failure, the Project Owner will inform EVN immediately 
and contract with the authorized party (belong to EVN) to verify/ calibrate and/or replace 
the failed power meter. 
G. Personnel Training

Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
91 
- In order to ensure a proper functioning of the project activity and a properly monitoring 
of emission reductions, the staff (VCS team) will be trained. The plant helpers will be 
trained in equipment operation, data recording, reports writing, operation and 
maintenance and emergency procedures in compliance with the monitoring plan.  
- During the operation period, the Project Owner will hold irregularly scheduled training to 
improve staff’s professional level 
- The new staff is not allowed to operate or maintain the equipment, monitoring system 
until they master the knowledge and skills after training mentioned above.  
H. The procedures for internal auditing and Quality assurance and quality control (QA/QC):  
- Internal auditing: This is an internal procedure to ensure the quality of operation and VCS 
monitoring. The meetings for internal audit are organized every year by the internal 
auditor. All the internal audits are recorded and stored.  
- QA/QC: The monitoring processes and all data collected as part of monitoring plan are 
double checked by internal auditor and VCS group manager. All the collected data are 
archived in both e lectronic and hard copy, and shall be kept until 2 years after the end 
of the crediting period, or the last issuance of VCUs, whichever is later.  
I.  The procedures for calibrating monitoring equipment  
- Weigh bridges: The weigh bridges  will be calibrated and verified comply with Viet Nam 
current regulation. As per Circular No. 07/2019/TT -BKHCN amendments to circular 
no. 23/2013/TT-BKHCN dated 26/09/2013 of Ministry  of Science and T echnology on 
measurement of group 2 measuring instrument , the calibration of weigh bridge s need to 
be implemented each 12 months.  
- Power meters: The meters will be calibrated and verified pursuant to national standard. 
According to According to the Decision No. 2739/QD -TDC on “promulgating metrological 
technical standard of Viet Nam ” issued by  Directorate for Standards, Metrology and 
Quality under Ministry of Science and Technology of Viet Nam on 23/12/2019, Article 2, 
the metrology standard –  DLVN 39:2019 takes effect from 01/01/2020. Paragraph 8.3, 
DLVN 39:2019 – “Alternating current static w att-hour meters  – Verification procedure”, 
the calibration for 3 phase meters need to be conducted every three years by the third 
party once during project  operation. After every calibration, the meters will be sealed so 
that no illegal interference is pos sible. 
- Wastewater flow meter: Flow meter will be calibrated and verified pursuant to National 
Standard. As per Circular No. 07/2019/TT -BKHCN amendments to circular 
no. 23/2013/TT-BKHCN dated 26/09/2013 of Ministry  of Science and T echnology on 
measurement of group 2 measuring instrument , the calibration of flow meter need to be 
implemented each 60 months.  
- Fuel meter ruler:  The ruler gauge will be calibrated at least once a year  as requirement 
of tool. 
- COD m eter: The monitoring instruments will be subject to regular maintenance and 
testing to ensure accuracy as per the manufacturer’s  instruction. 
 
K. Sampling approaching  
Not applicable .

1 PROJECT DETAILS 
1.1 Summary Description of the Project 
The Soc Son waste to power plant project (hereinafter “the project”) includes the construction 
and operation of a waste treatment plant, which is located at Nam Son waste treatment complex 
area, Soc Son District, Hanoi. The project is owned by Ha Noi Thien  Y Environmental Energy Joint 
Stock Company, which was established from 3 investors including Soc Son Renewable Energy 
Joint Stock Company, Perfect Wave Holdings Limited Company and Europe Tianying BVBA 
Company. 
The purpose of the project activity is to tr eat 4,000 tons of MSW per day to generate electricity 
with the maximum capacity of 75 MW. However, the plant is designed and installed with the 
capacity of 90 MW for the following reasons:  
- The nature of the waste to energy (WtE)  plant is unstable operation due to the harsh working 
conditions. The equipment requires more frequent maintenance/repair while the 
maintenance/repair time is relatively long compared to other conventional thermal power 
plants.  
- The waste treatment plant m ust be operated continuously. The reduction of operating 
capacity (partial shutdown of the plant for maintenance) will cause garbage congestion and 
environmental problems.  
Therefore, to ensure the waste treatment capacity as well as power generation capac ity, the WtE 
plant shall be able to have a backup capacity. The installation of 3 turbines/generators with 30 
MW per unit has considered the back -up capacity for the closing of one turbine/generator to 
maintain or repair. In this case, the aim of the back -up design is to ensure that the two remaining 
turbines/generators  (60 MW) are still capable of generating up to 80% of the required capacity  
of 75 MW . According to the FSR which was designed by the third party and also approved the 
national authority, with  4,000 tons of MSW per day, the maximum electricity capacity of the plant 
is 75 MW. Moreover, based on the signed power purchase agreement (PPA) between the PO and 
EVN, the maximum electricity capacity exported to the national grid is 75 MW. The electricit y 
capacity will be capped at 75MW by both parties EVN and the project owner through the 
Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) system during operation. Therefore, the actual 
capacity of 75 MW is used in the financial analysis.  
The annual estimate d electricity output  of the project activity  of 388.05 GWh is supplied to the 
national grid via the transmission line. The Soc Son waste to power plant will use 5 Waterleau 
continuous grate -type incinerators for treating MSW and a combination of 5 boilers, 3 steam 
turbines and 3 generators to recover heat from combustion of MSW for generating electricity.  
 Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
5 
Scenario existing prior to the implementation of project activity:  
Prior to the implementation of the pro ject activity, MSW treatment in Viet Nam was mainly 
landfilling without any  methane recovery system for flaring or energy purpose, therefore methane 
emissions from anaerobic decay of waste would have been released into the atmosphere.  
MSW of Ha Noi is mai nly treated at Nam Son landfill site  in Nam Son waste treatment area and 
Xuan Son landfill site  in Xuan Son waste treatment area, which are operated by URENCO Ha Noi 
– a public entity. If there is no project activity, the MSW will continue to be disposed a t Nam Son 
landfill site. Nam Son landfill site was a sanitary landfill, operated in 2000. Nam Son solid waste 
treatment complex area  spans over 157 ha, its construction is divided into  2 phases. Phase 1 of 
83 ha contained 10 cells and was closed. Phase 2 o f more than 73 ha includes 8 cells and 
technical infrastructure items.  After many expansions and phases, up to now, this landfill 
receives about 5,000 tons of MSW/day, accounting for 77% of MSW generated by Ha Noi.  
According to Decision No.609/QD -TTg issued by the Prime Minister dated April 25, 2014, on 
Approving the master plan on solid waste disposal of Ha Noi capital to 2030, with a vision to 
2050, the Nam Son  landfill is planned to expand  its capacity  to 257 ha by 2030 and 280 ha by 
2050
1. Nam Son landfill was constructed with a depth of greater 9 m, and equipped with 
mechanical compacting, levelling of the waste, and cover material of soil 2. T here is one LFG 
recovery project (at closed cells in Nam Son landfill) that was implemented under Clean 
Development Mechanism (CDM) 3. The LFG ca pture system was however abandoned due to the 
low price of Certified Emission Reductions (CERs) in the market. 
For electricity component, prior to the implementation of the project activity, the electricity in Viet 
Nam was produced mainly from fossil fuel sources and solely distributed to customers through 
the unique national electricity grid managed by the Vietnam Electricity (EVN).  
Baseline Scenario:  
The baseline scenario of the project activity is the same as the scenario existing prior to the start 
of implementation of the project activity. The project activity is the installation of a Greenfield 
MSW treatment plant, the baseline scenario is disposal  of the fresh waste in a solid waste 
disposal site (SWDS) without an LFG capture. 
For electricity generation, the baseline scenario is electricity delivered to the grid by the project 
activity would have  otherwise been generated by the operation of grid -connected power plants 
and by the addition of  new generation sources.

Estimated GHG emission reductions:  
The project activity helps to avoid methane release from SWDS(s) in the absence of the project 
activity and generate renewable electricity, which displaced part of the electricity otherwise 
supplied by grid connected fossil fuel fired power plants. Thus, GHG emission reductions can be 
achieved. The expected CO
2 emission reduction from the proposed project is 544 ,012 tCO2e 
annually and 3, 808,082 tCO2e in total over the first 7 years crediting period .

1) The seven Kyoto Protocol greenhouse gases.  
2) Ozone-depleting substances (ODS).

the methodology  development and review  process.

program, unless explicitly excluded.

document Jurisdictional and Nested REDD+ (JNR) Requirements.  
The project activity is to produce electricity from MSW that would have been left to decay 
anaerobically in waste landfills. The project will result in avoidance of quantity of methane (CH
4) 
that otherwise would have been released into the atmosphere due to the anaerobic decay of 
organic waste in landfills. Beside, the project will generate rene wable electricity, which displaced 
part of the electricity otherwise been supplied by grid connected fossil fuel fired power plants 
generating mainly CO
2 emission. Thus, GHG emission reductions can be achieved. This project 
type is not excluded from the Sc ope of VCS program as indicated in Table 1: Excluded project 
activities of VCS Standard, Version 4. 3. Therefore, the project activity is eligible to develop and 
register under the VCS Program .

1.11 Description of the Project Activity 
Prior to the implementation of the project activity, the same amount of MSW would be disposed 
at Nam Son landfill site. Nam Son landfill site was a sanitary landfill, operated in 2000. Currently, 
the landfill receives around 5,000 tons of MSW/day . As per Decision No.609 /QD-TTg on 
Approving the Master Plan on solid waste treatment of Hanoi to 2030, with a vision to 2050 
dated 25 April 2014, the  Nam Son  landfill is planned to  expand its capacity  to 6,000 tons per 
day by 2030 and 7,000 tons per day by 2050 (corresponding to an area of 257ha and 280ha by 
2030 and  2050, respectively) . The project activity is expected to use 4,000 tons of MS W/day. 
Therefore, the  Nam Son landfill area is sufficient  capacity to dispose waste with a comparable 
annual waste acceptance rate and with the same operating lifetime as the project activity.  
For electricity components, prior to the implementation of the project activity, the same amount 
of electricity would have been generated by the grid -connected power plants, which was  mainly 
from fossil fuel -based power plant.  The detailed information is mentioned in section 1.1 and 3.2 
on baseline scenario.  
The greenfield project activity involves the introduction  of the grate incineration technology to 
incinerate MSW at Hanoi city that would otherwise be disposed of at a local landfill site. The main 
equipment in the project activity includes 5 incinerators (800 ton of MSW per day), 5 boilers 
(65.8 ton per hour), 3 steam turbines and 3 generators (30 MW x 3 units), the transformer station 
and electricity transmission line, e xhaust gas cleaning system s and ash remove systems.   
After entering the treatment area through weighbridge s at the input gate, MSW will be moved to 
2 temporary waste bunkers, one is for  3 incineration lines, and another is for 2 remaining 
incineration lines. These tanks will be designed with maximum volume of 65,954 m 3 to store 
MSW within 8 -10 days. During storage in the waste bunkers, MSW will be turned and mixed to 
tear waste and remov e water to prevent anaerobic decomposition. Moreover, the air from the 
 Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
10 
waste bunkers will be blown to incinerator and used as a primary combustion air. MSW then 
provided to incinerators by clamshell buckets.  
Each moving grate incinerator is equipped a syst em, which feeding waste to grates, including a 
hopper, waste chute. Garbage is pushed into the combustion chamber by the system, then moved 
continuously and burned directly on the grate  in layer by layer, drying zone, burning zone and 
burn out zone. The un burnt components continue to move on the grate s to get out of the 
combustion chamber and fall into the slag collector  of furnace bottom. 
The heat recovered from  the incineration process will be provided to boilers. The superheated 
steam generated from the boiler s will be fed  into the steam turbine to generate kinetic energy . 
Turbines are connected to generators  to produce electricity. The electricity generated from the 
project activity will be exported to the national grid through the electricity transm ission line. 
The flue gases  from the incineration process including  NOx, SOx, HCl, heavy metal, dioxin, furan 
will be treated by the  exhausted gases control technology. This includes combustion control, 
including activated carbon adsorption, semi -dry deacidification, baghouse filter . After the treated 
clean gas, which meet s Viet Nam Environmental Quality Standard  will be release to the 
atmosphere from chimneys . The slag will be collected and transported to brick production plant. 
While fly ash will be solidified , then sent to the landfill site.    
The leachate generated from MSW bunkers will  be collected and gone  into the wastewater 
treatment system and is treated together with other wastewater sources by  the UASB technology 
to meet Viet Nam Environmental Quality Standard  before discharging the environment.

1.17 Sustainable Development Contributions 
The project contributes to the sustainable development on the local, regional, national scale in 
many aspects of environment, Sociality and Economy, as follows:  
Environmental benefits:  
• Instead of anaerobic decay of biodegradation waste that would have occurred in landfills, 
resulting in methane generation. The project activity will contribute to mitigation of 
greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions through incineration of the waste. The annual av erage 
expected emission reduction from the project activity is 544,012 tCO2e, which contributes 
to SDG  13 “Respond in a timely and effective manner to climate change and natural 
disaster” as per mentioned in Resolution on sustainable  development No 136/NQ -CP 
dated 25/09/2020 of The Viet nam Government. Emission reduction will be calculated at 
each monitoring period.  
• The Project activity would reduce the amount of waste going into the dumping site thus 
increasing the lifetime of the waste landfill sites. The purpose of this project activity is to 
treat average 4,000 ton of municipal solid waste (MSW) per day. Total annual amount of 
municipal sol id waste treated is estimated 1,460,000 ton , increasing quantity of waste 
at city treated by modern technology, preventing from disposing at landfill sites, which 
contributes to  Goal of SDG 11 “Promote sustainable and resilient urban and rural 
development; ensure safe living and working environments; ensure reasonable 
distribution of population and work force by region” as per mentioned in Resolution on 
sustainable development No 136/NQ -CP dated 25/09/2020 of The Viet nam 
Government. The quantity of municip al waste treated will be measured continuously by 
weighbridge and gathered at each monitoring period.  
 Project Description: VCS Version 4. 1 
16 
• Contributing to prevent the soil, air and water pollution and improve Environment quality 
for local residents.  
• Through recovering heat from incineration a nd generating clean power, the project also 
contributes to mitigate exhausted gases (GHG included), which would otherwise have 
been released into atmosphere because of production of electricity from fossil-fuel power 
plants. 
Economic benefits:  
• During the o peration phase, the project activity is estimated to supply 388.05 GWh  of 
renewable energy to the national grid annual, which contributes to SDG 7 “Ensure access 
to sustainable, reliable and affordable energy for all” as per mentioned in Resolution on 
sustainable development No 136/NQ -CP dated 25/09/2020 of The Viet nam 
Government.  
• Advantage of incineration is that treat MSW thoroughly, minimize volume of waste, save 
land area to use other economic purposes instead of MSW landfill treatment.  
Social benefits : 
• Introducing the advanced waste to power technology on solid waste treatment that is not 
yet commonly applied in Viet Nam.  
• Creating jobs during the construction and the operation phase and contribute to 
improving income for people. During the operation phase, the project activity is estimated 
to create approximately 180 jobs, which contributes  to SDG  8 “ Ensure sustained, 
inclusive and sustainable economic growth; full and productive employment and decent 
work for all” as per mentioned in Resolution on sustainable development No 136/NQ -CP 
dated 25/09/2020 of The Viet nam Government. List of employees at the plant will be 
monitored and provided at each monitoring period.  
• Improving the technical skills of workers via trainings to operate the w aste treatment 
plant.

commune grievance submissions.

local authority and the residents. The comment boo ks will be securely placed in the 
chosen locations and daily checked by responsible persons. Administrative Department
